planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Beefmaster requires a very sturdy support system — heavy cages or strong staking — due to the extreme weight of mature fruit clusters.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Beefmaster is one of the most popular extra-large slicing tomato hybrids in American home gardens — an indeterminate hybrid producing truly giant fruits averaging 16–32 ounces (1–2 pounds) or more per fruit, with the classic beefsteak form: large, meaty, low-seed, deep-red flesh with rich, balanced tomato flavor that makes it the premier choice for thick-sliced tomatoes for sandwiches and BLTs. The extremely heavy fruit size requires robust support — the vines need strong cages or stakes and should have the heaviest fruit clusters supported or tied as they develop to prevent stem breakage. Beefmaster was developed to combine maximum fruit size with disease resistance (V, F, N, T, A resistance package) that most older beefsteak heirlooms lack, making it significantly more reliable in warm, humid Gulf South climates. In zone 9, Beefmaster performs best in the spring window (February–May transplanting) when temperatures allow the large fruits to develop without excessive heat during fruit set — temperatures above 95°F can cause blossom drop and inhibit fruit development on large-fruited varieties. Consistent, deep watering reduces blossom-end rot, which can affect the large-fruited types more than small-fruited varieties.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in well-drained, loose, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 9, sow September–November for the main fall crop and January–February for a spring crop; succession sow every 2 weeks for a continuous harvest.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in well-drained, loose, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade during cool seasons; French Breakfast is a classic European heirloom radish with a history dating to the early nineteenth century — producing oblong-to-cylindrical, 3–4 inch roots with a distinctive two-tone color pattern: brilliant scarlet-red over the upper two-thirds grading to crisp white at the tip. The elongated, elegant shape is distinctly different from the standard round red radish and makes French Breakfast instantly recognizable in the garden and at market. The flavor is mild and crisp — somewhat milder than globe radishes when harvested young, with a light, clean pepper-radish flavor that becomes stronger and more pungent if roots are left in the ground too long or grown during warm weather. Traditional French usage was to eat them dipped in butter with a pinch of salt at breakfast — hence the name. In zone 9, French Breakfast radishes are a cool-season crop performing best September through April; they bolt and become pungent in the summer heat. Succession sow every 2 weeks for continuous harvest. Thin to 2–3 inches for the best root development. Harvest when roots reach 3/4–1 inch diameter for the mildest flavor.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in late winter to early spring in very well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, sandy loam in full sun; plant at least two cultivars for cross-pollination; Patriot requires 800–1000 chill hours and is best suited to zone 8 and colder rather than the warmer portions of zone 9.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full sun with at least one other blueberry cultivar for cross-pollination; Patriot is a cold-hardy northern highbush blueberry cultivar developed by the University of Maine — selected for extreme cold hardiness (to -20°F), compact upright habit, and good resistance to root rot (Phytophthora cinnamomi), which makes it more adaptable to heavier or less-than-perfectly-drained soils than most highbush cultivars. The large, firm, aromatic, medium-blue berries ripen in early-to-midseason (July in zone 5–6) with a sweet, excellent blueberry flavor and very good shelf life. Patriot is widely grown in the Northeast and upper Midwest where its cold hardiness is the decisive advantage. In zone 8 it performs well with sufficient soil acidification — a reliable producer where winter temperatures regularly drop below 10°F. In zone 9, Patriot's 800–1000 chill hour requirement is not reliably met in the warmest locations; southern highbush cultivars (Misty, O'Neal, Sharpblue) are better adapted to zone 9. Patriot is a good pollination partner for Bluecrop, Blueray, and other midseason northern highbush varieties. Amend soil with peat moss, sulfur, or acidifying fertilizers to maintain the essential low pH.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in late winter to early spring in very well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, sandy loam in full sun; plant with another low-chill southern highbush (O'Neal, Sharpblue, Emerald) for cross-pollination; Misty requires only 150–300 chill hours and excels in zone 8b–10.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, sandy loam in full sun with another southern highbush cultivar for cross-pollination; Misty is an outstanding low-chill southern highbush blueberry cultivar developed by the University of Florida — requiring only 150–300 chill hours, making it one of the best blueberry choices for the Gulf Coast, Central Florida, and other warm-winter regions where northern highbush varieties consistently fail to produce. Misty is additionally distinctive for having attractive ornamental value beyond its fruit production — it has an elegant, upright habit with attractive dark-green foliage that turns red in fall, and in spring produces showy clusters of white to pink, urn-shaped flowers before leaf-out. The medium to large, light-blue fruits ripen in April through May in zone 9, with a somewhat mild but pleasant blueberry flavor. As a very early ripening variety (among the earliest of all southern highbush types in zone 9), Misty gives Gulf Coast gardeners fresh blueberries in April when markets charge premium prices. Plant Misty in raised beds or in containers with acidified peat-based potting mix — most Gulf Coast clay and native soils are too alkaline and must be heavily amended or blueberries will fail regardless of cultivar. Pair with O'Neal or Sharpblue for best cross-pollination and maximum yields.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to deep shade; Silvery Sunproof is one of the few variegated liriopes that maintains its bright cream-and-green color in full sun without bleaching — most variegated liriopes require partial shade for best color.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to deep shade; Silvery Sunproof is the premier variegated sun-tolerant liriope — a striking selection with bold, upright, strap-like leaves striped in green and cream-yellow that maintains its vivid variegation in full sun conditions where most variegated liriopes bleach to pale yellowish white. The ability to perform in full sun without color loss gives Silvery Sunproof greater landscape versatility than variegated competitors, making it effective as edging, ground cover, or accent plant in both shaded and fully exposed positions. In late summer, the plants produce showy spikes of bright lilac-purple flowers above the variegated foliage, providing an attractive contrast to the cream-and-green leaves. Dark blue-black berries follow the flowers and persist into winter, providing additional ornamental interest. Like all liriopes, Silvery Sunproof forms slowly expanding clumps that fill in over 2–3 seasons and tolerate the full range of Gulf South conditions — heat, humidity, drought once established, clay soil, and root competition under trees. In zone 9, it performs reliably as an evergreen to semi-evergreen ground cover and edging plant. Shear to 3–4 inches in February before new growth emerges to remove winter-tattered foliage and encourage fresh, colorful new leaves.
